name: Swyx
summary: Enterprise agent adoption depends on compliance and governance readiness, not just coding speed. He also highlighted fast-rising demand for coding agents beyond engineering roles and referenced strong Devin growth momentum.

name: Peter Yang
summary: Compared OpenClaw vs Claude Code in day-to-day use (especially remote control and permissions) and solicited practitioner feedback on role split. He also previewed interviews with builders behind Claude Cowork and Codex.

name: Thariq
summary: Shared a new Claude Code /init experiment that interviews users and bootstraps repo setup (Claude.md, hooks, skills) based on customer feedback, reducing friction for agent-ready repo standardization.

name: Guillermo Rauch
summary: Argued that code is increasingly an output rather than the primary input, and demonstrated the thesis with a v0-generated browser 3D Jenga project covering procedural assets and interaction details.

name: Aaron Levie
summary: Said enterprises are still very early in agent deployment: most have not scaled coding agents yet, let alone broader knowledge-work agents, indicating a sizable execution gap.

name: Garry Tan
summary: Open-sourced GStack and framed Codex-like assistants as support across office hours and review workflows. Also previewed Claude Code in Chrome Sidebar via GStack integration.

name: Zara Zhang
summary: Introduced the 'codebase to course' skill that converts codebases into interactive learning flows with visualization and plain-English explanations, signaling agent use for onboarding and knowledge transfer.

name: Peter Steinberger
summary: Reported major harness testing speed improvements from about 10 minutes to around 2 minutes, indicating rapid tooling reliability and performance hardening.

name: Dan Shipper
summary: Proposed a two-person AI-native engineering pod model ('pirate + architect') to balance fast shipping through vibe coding with architectural integrity and long-term maintainability.

name: Aditya Agarwal
role: General Partner at South Park Commons; ex-CTO at Dropbox
summary: Reflected on founder-capital psychology: some teams use funding as security, while others view capital as optional once conviction and execution are strong.
